sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a three-day history of a hacking cough that started with a gradual onset and has progressed to include coughing up green phlegm.
The cough is accompanied by a sore throat, nasal congestion, and no fever, shortness of breath, body aches, nausea, or vomiting.
The patient has a history of seasonal allergies, which are typically seasonal and present around spring time.
The patient has been managing their diabetes with metformin 500mg daily, which has been tolerated well, and has been checking their blood sugars with readings in the range of 120.
The patient has a history of hypertension and is currently on hydrochlorothiazide, which has been taken as prescribed.
The patient's diet has been a challenge, particularly with sugar intake.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and seasonal allergies.
Review of systems: The patient denies fever, shortness of breath, body aches, nausea, or vomiting.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg daily for diabetes
Hydrochlorothiazide taken as prescribed for hypertension
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is elevated at 144/72, otherwise vital signs are within normal limits.
Physical exam: The patient's sinuses are tender to palpation bilaterally.
The pharynx is erythematous and the tonsils are symmetrical without exudates.
The patient has anterior cervical adenopathy.
The lungs are clear to auscultation.
The heart has a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.
The lower extremities are without edema.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an upper respiratory infection, likely caused by a virus, given the sudden onset and the fact that they were exposed to someone with similar symptoms.
Problem list: Upper respiratory infection; Type 2 diabetes; Hypertension; Seasonal allergies.
Plan:
I prescribed Coricidin HBP for the cough and advised the patient to continue drinking plenty of fluids and getting plenty of rest.
I will check the patient for COVID-19 and will provide results.
I increased the hydrochlorothiazide dose to 25mg once daily and advised the patient to take it in the morning.
I also recommended that the patient continue to check their blood pressure at different times of the day.
Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up visit to check their hemoglobin A1c and to reassess their blood pressure.
